content.text: File and folder access is the most frequently-used capability in Cowork tasks. When you ask the engine to read a report, produce a summary document, search a codebase, or update a spreadsheet, every one of those instructions resolves to a file operation. Understanding where Cowork looks for files, what it can read, and how permissions govern writes is essential for building tasks that work reliably without surprises.
File Access Architecture

attrs.alt: Cowork file access architecture: Project workspace on the left connected to the Agentic Engine in the center, which also connects to cloud connectors (Google Drive, GitHub, Slack, Jira) on the right
attrs.title: Cowork File Access Architecture

content.text: Cowork reads and writes files through two distinct surfaces: the
Project workspace
(a local folder managed by the desktop app) and
OAuth connectors

(Google Drive, GitHub, Slack, and Jira). Both surfaces expose the same file tool API to the agentic engine; the difference is where the files physically live and what permissions govern each operation.
The Project Workspace

Every Cowork Project has a private workspace folder on your local machine. All files you upload to the Project are stored here, and all files the engine writes during task execution land here by default. The engine refers to files in this folder by their base name — it does not need the full OS path.
Workspace location on disk
Platform
Default path
macOS
~/Library/Application Support/Cowork/Projects/<project-id>/
Windows
%APPDATA%\Cowork\Projects\<project-id>\

You can open this folder in Finder/Explorer directly from the Project settings panel. Files written by the engine appear there as ordinary files; you can open, edit, and version-control them like any other local file.
Uploading files to a Project
There are three ways to put a file into the workspace so the engine can read it:
Drag and drop
into the Project Files panel in the Cowork desktop app.
Attach at task creation
— click the paper-clip icon in the task compose window. The file is copied into the workspace and automatically visible to the engine for that task.
Copy files directly
into the workspace folder using Finder or Explorer. Cowork detects new files within a few seconds and indexes them.
Workspace file size limits
Per-file maximum:
50 MB for uploaded files via the UI. Larger files can be placed in the workspace folder directly.
Total workspace:
No hard limit on Pro/Team (disk-bound), but the engine's context window constrains how much content from those files it can hold in memory at once.
Engine reading limit:
When the engine reads a file, the extracted text is added to the context window. For very large files, the engine reads in chunks and summarizes earlier chunks as the context fills.
File Format Support
The engine reads files by extracting their text content. Not every file format yields useful text:
Format
Support level
Notes
Plain text (.txt, .md, .csv, .json, .yaml)
Full
Read and written natively; engine sees exact content
PDF
Good
Text is extracted; layout and images are not preserved. Scanned PDFs (image-only) yield no usable text.
Word (.docx)
Good
Body text extracted; complex tables and embedded objects may be simplified
Excel (.xlsx, .csv)
Good
Cells read as tabular text; formulas are shown as their computed values
PowerPoint (.pptx)
Partial
Slide text and speaker notes are extracted; charts and images are not
Images (.png, .jpg, .webp)
Vision
Sent to the model's vision capability; engine can describe, extract text (OCR), or analyze the image content
Binary / archive (.zip, .exe, .bin)
None
Binary content cannot be extracted as meaningful text; engine will report an error or skip the file
File System Tool Calls
The engine interacts with the workspace through a small set of file tools. Understanding each tool helps you write task prompts that direct the engine to the right action.
file_read
Reads the content of a single file and adds it to the context window as an observation. The engine typically calls this when it needs the content of a specific file you referenced in the prompt.

Prompt: "Summarize the key risks in Q3-board-report.pdf"

Engine:
  tool_call: file_read("Q3-board-report.pdf")
  observation: [text extracted from PDF — 12,400 tokens]
  → reasoning: identifying and summarizing risk sections
  → output: 5-bullet executive risk summary
file_write
Creates or overwrites a file in the workspace. This is an "Ask" permission action by default — the engine will pause and request approval unless you configured file writes to Auto for this Project.

Prompt: "Write the risk summary to risk-summary.md"

Engine:
  tool_call: file_write("risk-summary.md", "## Q3 Risk Summary\n...")
  permission_request: [pauses — shows user the file name and size]
  [user approves]
  observation: file_write succeeded
  → output: "risk-summary.md has been written to the workspace."
Tip:
To suppress the permission prompt for trusted write destinations, add the file name or pattern to the Project's Auto-approve list in Settings → Permissions → File writes.
list_directory

Lists the files in the workspace (or a subfolder). The engine calls this when it needs to discover what files are available — typically when your prompt references a file that the engine cannot find by name.

Prompt: "Read the Q3 report"

Engine:
  tool_call: file_read("Q3 report")  ← guessed name
  observation: Error — file not found
  tool_call: list_directory(".")
  observation: ["Q3-2024-board-report.pdf", "budget.xlsx", "notes.md"]
  tool_call: file_read("Q3-2024-board-report.pdf")  ← correct name
  observation: [PDF text]
file_move and file_delete

Move renames or relocates a file within the workspace. Delete removes it permanently. Both require "Ask" permission by default. The engine uses these when you ask it to organize, archive, or clean up workspace files.

Prompt: "Move all processed reports from the inbox/ folder to archive/"

Engine:
  tool_call: list_directory("inbox/")
  observation: ["jan-report.pdf", "feb-report.pdf", "mar-report.pdf"]
  tool_call: file_move("inbox/jan-report.pdf", "archive/jan-report.pdf")
  tool_call: file_move("inbox/feb-report.pdf", "archive/feb-report.pdf")
  tool_call: file_move("inbox/mar-report.pdf", "archive/mar-report.pdf")
  output: "Moved 3 files from inbox/ to archive/"
Permission Model for File Operations
Every file tool call is subject to the Project's permission policy. The defaults follow a simple read/write split:
Operation
Default tier
When to change
file_read
Auto
Restrict to Ask if the workspace contains sensitive files you want to audit before the engine reads
file_write (new file)
Ask
Promote to Auto for scheduled tasks that produce output files overnight
file_write (overwrite existing)
Ask
Keep at Ask if the target file is important; promote to Auto only for generated output files
file_delete
Ask
Rarely promote to Auto; deletion is irreversible and best reviewed
file_move
Ask
Promote to Auto for filing/archiving pipelines when destinations are well-defined
Cloud Connectors
In addition to the local workspace, Cowork can read from and write to cloud services via OAuth connectors. Connectors are authorized once per Project and then available to any task in that Project.
Google Drive
The Drive connector grants the engine access to specific folders you authorize during setup. It can:
Read Google Docs, Sheets, Slides, and uploaded PDFs/DOCX files
List folder contents to discover available files
Create new Google Docs or upload files (Ask permission required)
Update existing Google Docs content (Ask permission required)

Prompt: "Read the Q3 OKR document from the 'Strategy 2024' folder in Drive
         and produce a progress report."

Engine:
  tool_call: drive_list_folder("Strategy 2024")
  observation: ["Q3 OKRs.gdoc", "Q2 OKRs.gdoc", "Annual Strategy.gdoc"]
  tool_call: drive_read("Strategy 2024/Q3 OKRs.gdoc")
  observation: [Google Doc text — OKR table with status column]
  → output: progress report markdown
GitHub
The GitHub connector gives the engine read access to authorized repositories and write access (with Ask permission) to create issues, PRs, and file changes. Common use cases:
Read source files, README, or configuration files from a repo
Summarize recent commits or open pull requests
Create a GitHub Issue from a task's output (Ask permission)
Post a PR review comment (Ask permission)

Prompt: "Read CHANGELOG.md from the api-server repo and produce
         a customer-facing release summary for v2.4.0."

Engine:
  tool_call: github_read_file(repo="api-server", path="CHANGELOG.md")
  observation: [changelog text with v2.4.0 section]
  → output: customer-facing release notes markdown
Connector authorization scope

Authorization is scoped at connector setup time. The engine cannot access Drive folders, GitHub repos, Slack channels, or Jira boards that were not included in the scope you authorized. This is intentional — it prevents one Project's engine from reading data meant for another Project.
Connector
Authorization granularity
Adding more scope
Google Drive
Specific folders and their subfolders
Re-open the connector settings and select additional folders
GitHub
Specific repositories within your GitHub account/org
Re-run the OAuth flow and select additional repos in GitHub's permission dialog
Slack
Specific channels
Add channels in connector settings; Slack workspace admin must approve
Jira
Specific boards / project keys
Select additional boards in connector settings
Common File Access Patterns
Pattern 1: Read → Analyze → Write output
The most common pattern. Upload source files to the workspace, run a task that reads them, write the result to a new file.

Project files: customer-calls-q3.txt, customer-calls-q4.txt

Prompt: "Read both customer call transcripts and identify the top 5 product
         complaints that appear in both quarters. Write the findings to
         complaints-analysis.md with supporting quotes."

Result: complaints-analysis.md written to workspace
Pattern 2: Connector read → Workspace write
Pull data from a cloud connector and produce a local file.

Connector: Google Drive folder "Board Materials"

Prompt: "Read the last 3 board decks from the 'Board Materials' Drive folder
         and produce a one-page briefing document showing how our KPIs have
         trended. Save to kpi-trend-briefing.md."

Result: kpi-trend-briefing.md written to workspace
Pattern 3: Multi-file batch processing
Use a scheduled task to process all files in a folder on a recurring basis.

Project files: invoices/01.pdf, invoices/02.pdf, invoices/03.pdf, ...

Prompt (scheduled daily): "List all PDFs in the invoices/ folder. For each one
                           not yet in processed.json, extract vendor name, amount,
                           and due date. Append the extracted data to processed.json
                           and move the PDF to invoices/done/."

Result: processed.json updated; each PDF moved to done/
Pattern 4: Task chaining via files
When a task is too large for one context window, chain tasks: Task A writes intermediate output to a file; Task B reads that file as its input.

Task A prompt: "Read all 12 monthly sales CSVs in the data/ folder. Compute
               total revenue, units sold, and margin per product. Write the
               aggregated table to sales-summary.json."

Task B prompt: "Read sales-summary.json. Identify the top 5 products by margin
               and the bottom 5 by margin. Write an executive commentary to
               margin-analysis.md explaining the gap and recommending actions."
Troubleshooting File Access Issues
Symptom
Likely cause
Fix
Engine says "file not found" for a file you uploaded
Name in prompt doesn't match actual file name
Use the exact file name including extension in your prompt; check capitalization
PDF read returns empty or garbled text
Scanned PDF (image-only), or PDF with password protection
Use OCR software to convert to searchable PDF; remove password before uploading
file_write pauses for approval every run
Write permission is set to Ask; needed for scheduled/overnight tasks
Promote the output file pattern to Auto in Project settings → Permissions
Drive connector says "folder not found"
Folder not included in connector authorization scope
Open connector settings, add the missing folder, re-authorize if prompted
Engine reads old version of a file
Workspace index cache is stale after a recent file replacement
Wait 10–15 seconds after replacing a file; or re-upload through the UI
Large Excel file produces incomplete data
File is too large for a single context window
Export only the relevant sheet/columns as a CSV before uploading; or split processing across multiple tasks
Writing Files Safely: Overwrites and Atomicity

Every file_write call fully replaces the target file. Cowork does not merge or append by default — if the engine calls file_write on a path that already contains data, that data is overwritten. Understanding this is important when designing tasks that update files incrementally.
Safe overwrite pattern

: read the existing file first, construct the full updated content in context, then write the result back in one call. The engine holds the complete document in its context window before touching disk, so no data is silently lost.
Example task prompt demonstrating a safe incremental update:

1. Read /workspace/reports/budget-tracker.csv
2. Parse the existing rows
3. Append this new expense row:
   2026-06-30, Software, $149.00, Cowork subscription
4. Write the complete updated CSV back to budget-tracker.csv
Because the write happens after a full read, there is no risk of silently discarding earlier rows. Three practices keep file writes predictable:
Name output files explicitly.
Specify the full destination path (e.g., /workspace/reports/summary-2026-06.md) rather than asking the engine to choose a name. This prevents accidental overwrites of existing files.
Use versioned filenames for drafts.
When iterating on a document, instruct the engine to write draft-v1.md, draft-v2.md, and so on, rather than overwriting a single file. The workspace is not version-controlled.
Enable the Ask permission tier for writes in sensitive directories.
If a folder contains the source of truth for important records, set file_write to Ask so you confirm each overwrite in the activity log.
Working with Structured Data Files

Cowork can read and transform structured data formats — CSV, JSON, YAML, and TOML — entirely within the engine's context window without installing extra dependencies. The engine parses the raw text, processes it in memory, and writes the result back. This makes it practical for lightweight ETL-style tasks directly from a Project workspace.
JSON transformation
Reading a JSON file, reshaping its structure, and writing it back is a common one-step task:

Read /workspace/data/users.json. It is an array of objects with fields
{id, firstName, lastName, email, createdAt}.

Produce a new file /workspace/data/users-export.json with this shape:
{id, fullName, email, signupDate}
where fullName = firstName + " " + lastName
and signupDate = createdAt formatted as YYYY-MM-DD.
CSV aggregation
For CSV files with a moderate number of rows (up to a few thousand), the engine can group, sum, and filter within its context window:

Read /workspace/data/expenses-june.csv (columns: date, category, amount, note).

Group by category and sum the amount column for each group.
Write a summary CSV to /workspace/data/expenses-june-by-category.csv
with columns: category, total_amount, transaction_count.
Size limit for in-context data processing

: the engine loads the entire file into its context window to parse it. CSV files beyond approximately 5,000–10,000 rows will consume a significant portion of the available context budget. For large datasets, consider pre-filtering the file to the relevant rows before passing it to a Cowork task, or use a connector like GitHub Actions or a scheduled Python script to pre-aggregate.
YAML and configuration files
YAML is particularly useful for configuration management tasks. The engine can read a config file, apply a targeted change, and write it back while preserving the remaining structure:

Read /workspace/config/app.yaml.
Update the value of logging.level from "info" to "debug".
Leave all other keys unchanged.
Write the result back to the same path.

Because YAML is plain text, the engine can surgically update a single nested key without a parser library. This is safer than using sed-style substitution, which can match unintended occurrences of the same string.
File Access Across Task Runs

The Project workspace persists between task runs. Files written during one task are immediately available to the next task in the same Project. This makes the workspace an effective handoff point between sequential tasks that would each exceed a single context window.
Scenario
Persistence behavior
Notes
Files you upload to a Project
Persist until you delete them
Available to all tasks in the same Project
Files written by the engine
Persist in the workspace folder
Can be inputs to the next scheduled task
Connector files (Drive, GitHub)
Fetched live at task execution time
Not cached between runs; changes in the source are picked up automatically
Task context window
Cleared after each task run
Files in the workspace are the durable record; context is ephemeral

A scheduled daily task can, for example, write a JSON file containing the previous day's metrics. The next day's run reads that file to produce a rolling 7-day trend without needing to re-fetch the historical data from external sources. The workspace acts as a durable, low-overhead inter-task message bus.
Summary

Cowork's file access system has two tiers: the Project workspace (a local folder managed by the desktop app, scoped to your machine) and OAuth connectors (Google Drive, GitHub, Slack, Jira), which grant access to cloud data within authorized boundaries. Read operations are Auto by default; write, move, and delete are Ask — meaning the loop pauses for approval unless you explicitly promote them for scheduled runs. The engine reads most document formats well (PDF, DOCX, XLSX, plain text, images via vision), and fails gracefully on binary or scanned formats. Knowing these mechanics lets you design tasks that reference files correctly, configure permissions for unattended execution, and chain tasks through shared workspace files for workflows too large for a single context window.
